sections.title: A: Review Vowel Sounds and Digraphs

steps.text: In this book, we'll always start by reviewing the vowel sounds. Which ones are the vowels?
Student taps A, E, I, O, U and says sounds.
Why are there 2 letters on 1 tile?
Expected: Because those 2 letters make just 1 sound.
That's right. Two letters that make just 1 sound are called Digraphs. Show me the other digraphs.
Student points to the TH, SH, CK, and WH tiles and makes their sounds.
Let's take a look at your extra practice page. Go ahead and read the words to me.
Review homework for accuracy.
Now I'll say a nonsense word, and you repeat it. Ready? CHUP.
Now say just the FIRST sound in that word.
Dictate: THED, WHOF, SHAV, VIM
Now we're going to switch. This time, I want the LAST sound. Ready? LISH.
What's the LAST sound?
Dictate: YUTCH, GATH, MOX, NUG
Now for the MIDDLE sound—the vowel. Say both the sound AND its keyword. Ready? CHAM.
What's the MIDDLE sound?
Dictate: MOG, PEZ, THUN, TIB

sections.title: D: NEW TEACHING #1: Read Words with Blends

steps.text: Let's start working on words. Touch-and-say each sound. Now slowly blend those sounds together.
Expected: /l/ - /a/ - /sh/ -> Lash

prompts: Touch and say your sounds again.
Does your vowel match the keyword Apple or Eddy?
Is there a Digraph at the end?

steps.text: How many sounds in that word?
Answer: 3
But how many actual letters in that word?
Answer: 4
Why are there more letters than sounds?
Answer: SH is a Digraph. Those 2 letters make only 1 sound.
But sometimes 4 letters WILL make four separate sounds. Let me show you what I mean.
Touch-and-say those sounds, then slowly blend them into a word. Is there a Digraph in that word now?
Answer: No.

You're right. But there's something new in that word. There are two consonants next to each other... Whenever there are two consonants next to each other, and each makes its own sound, it is called a Blend.
What is the word now?
Answer: List
And where's the Blend?
Answer: At the end -- the ST

sections.title: F: Spell Real Words with Fingers, then Tiles

steps.text: Now it's your turn to build words with tiles. The first word is MUST. Repeat that word.
Now say it again, very slowly. Use your fingers to break it into sounds.
Student raises one finger per sound: /m/ /u/ /s/ /t/
Pull down a tile for each finger, while making its sound.
To double-check, slowly run your finger below those tiles while blending those sounds together.
Now change just one letter... to make that word say MIST.
Dictate: LIFT, LEFT, HELP, HELD, RENT, REST, LOST, COST, CAST, FELT, MELT, MELD, DAMP, DUMP, THUMP

sections.title: G: Read Nonsense Words on Tiles

steps.text: Now I'm going to build a nonsense word. Touch-and-say each sound.
Student touches R and says /r/, touches O and says /o/, touches P and says /p/, touches T and says /t/.
Slowly blend those sounds together. Now say it fast like a word.
Tutor builds: THACT, CHIST, ZUND, QUENT

sections.title: H: Spell Nonsense Words with Fingers, then Tiles

steps.text: Now you'll spell some nonsense words the same way you spelled real words. Ready? The first word is CHOFT.
Say it slowly. Use your fingers to break it into sounds.
Pull down a tile for each of those sounds.
Dictate: THANT, SHUPT, BISP, JELM

sections.title: I: Read Words Through Word Frame

steps.text: Now you're going to read words using a word frame.
Give the Lesson 1: Read These Words page and the word frame to the student.
If you know that word for sure, just say it. Otherwise, touch-and-say each sound, then blend them together.
Student reads through the lists. Real words in the first two columns, nonsense words in the third.

sections.title: J: Spell Words with Fingers, then Paper

steps.text: Now you're going to spell words on paper. First you'll do finger spelling. Ready? The word is BEND. Repeat that word.
Say it again, slowly. Use your fingers to break it into sounds.
Student says sounds on fingers.
Now make each sound as you write the letter.
As a last step, double-check what you wrote. To do that, first blend the sounds together. Now say it fast like a word.
Dictate Real Words: BEND, THEFT, HUNT, POND, SHELF, HAND
Dictate Nonsense Words: TASP, SEPT, WHOMP, BIFT, NUST

sections.title: K: Read FIRST Sight Word List

steps.text: You're doing really well on words that you can sound out. But there are some words in our language that you can NOT sound out. These are called Sight words.
Student reads down each column. Keep track of correct and incorrect responses.

sections.title: L: Create Sight Word Spelling Cards

steps.text: Now I'm going to ask you to spell those Sight words. I'm hoping there are a few you don't know how to spell...so we'll have something to practice.
Dictate each sight word from the list. Stop when the student has misspelled 3 words.
OK, I found 3 that are tricky. So let's stop and make cards for them.
Create cards with tricky letter in red. Have student practice 'See and spell' on the wall, then 'See and write' on the table, then on paper.
Now you'll read some phrases... that tell Who, Did What, or Where.
Who phrases: His cast, Kent, The tent, Milt, The ump, The last gal
Did What phrases: has a list, got wet, was lost, did his best, ran fast, went west
Where phrases: at camp, in his hand, to the ramp, on the big test, in the pond, on the sand
Go ahead and pick a WHO and a DID WHAT phrase and make a short sentence.

sections.title: N: Spell Phrases and Sight Words on Paper

steps.text: Now you're going to write three phrases. The first phrase is HAD A DENT. Repeat that phrase.
Go ahead and write that phrase. Use finger spelling to help with the tough words.
Double check by reading exactly what you wrote. Any word that you want help checking?
Dictate: LEND A HAND, MAN WITH A LIMP
Dictate Sight Words from the Spelling deck.
Now let's look at some sentences. Read that first sentence to yourself. Now read it out loud.
1. The tent got wet in the pond.
Go ahead and mark each phrase. Mark the WHO phrase. Mark the DID WHAT phrase. Mark the WHERE phrase.
Now read that sentence with phrasing. That means put a small pause between each phrase.
Have student read and mark each ODD-numbered sentence on the page.
Now you're going to write three sentences. The first sentence is THE RED VAN HAD A DENT IN IT. Repeat that sentence.
Check your sentence by reading exactly what you wrote. Here's the first question I'm always going to ask: does it start with a capital and end with punctuation?
Dictate: DID MEG LEND A HAND?, THE MAN WITH A LIMP RAN FAST.
Now you're ready to read a story. Preread the story. That means read it to yourself first. Sound out any words you're not sure of. Then read it out loud to me.
Student reads 'The Gift', 'Fish', 'The Lump', or 'Dent in His Rig'. Ask comprehension questions.
